head_coach.name: Shane Steichen
head_coach.coaching_tree: Norv Turner → Chargers lineage → Nick Sirianni/Eagles influence. Steichen broke into the NFL under Norv Turner with the Chargers (2011), worked under Turner again in Cleveland (2013), then rose through the Chargers' offensive staff (2014-2020) working alongside Frank Reich and Nick Sirianni. Became Eagles OC under Sirianni (2021-2022) before being hired as Colts HC in 2023.
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Run-first foundation with RPO-heavy passing attack. Self-described philosophy: 'throw to score points and run to win.' Steichen is a play-caller who adapts his offense to his QB's strengths — built around Hurts' legs in Philadelphia, around Jones' pre-snap processing in Indianapolis. Heavy use of pre-snap motion to diagnose coverage, play-action/bootleg concepts, and inside/split zone run game. Aggressive on fourth downs and situationally creative.

offensive_coordinator.name: Jim Bob Cooter
offensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: Peyton Manning/Colts lineage → Detroit Lions OC under Jim Caldwell → Jets RB coach → Eagles consultant (worked with Steichen) → Jaguars PGC under Doug Pederson → Colts OC. Cooter's foundational offensive education came working with Manning in Indianapolis (2009-2011) and Denver (2013). His scheme roots blend Manning's quick-passing, pre-snap diagnosis concepts with the power-blocking schemes he reverted to in Detroit.
offensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: In Detroit, switched from zone-blocking to power/man-blocking and installed quick-passing concepts (high-percentage, short-to-intermediate throws) that masked OL deficiencies. As Jets RB coach, deepened his run-game knowledge. As Eagles consultant, worked directly with Steichen on scheme integration. In Jacksonville, helped improve passing efficiency. With the Colts, Cooter does not call plays — Steichen retains playcalling — but Cooter is deeply involved in weekly game-planning and offensive installation.
offensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Helped Matthew Stafford produce the most efficient 8-game stretch of his career in Detroit. Worked with Le'Veon Bell to produce 1,250 scrimmage yards with the Jets. His bedrock philosophy is to de-stress the quarterback through quick reads and simplified progressions. Less proven as a developer of young QBs — his best work has been with established veterans (Stafford, Manning).

defensive_coordinator.name: Lou Anarumo
defensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: DB coaching background (Purdue, Miami, NYG) → Miami Dolphins interim DC under Dan Campbell (2015) → Bengals DC (2019-2024) → Colts DC (2025-present). Not from a traditional coaching tree — Anarumo is self-made, blending Kevin Coyle's secondary fundamentals with his own innovative approach. His one-year overlap with James Bettcher (3-4 principles) on the 2018 Giants also influenced his front flexibility. He draws from everywhere and synthesizes, rather than belonging to one lineage.
defensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: In Cincinnati, Anarumo evolved from a struggling unit (2019-2020) into a top-10 defense (2021-2022) through creative coverage disguises and safety rotation concepts. Led the league in drop-8 coverage for three straight seasons. Pioneered 'walking linebacker' usage and 'Locked' coverages before they became league trends. By 2023-2024, was shifting toward five-man pressures with Quarters (Palms) behind them. In Indianapolis (2025), fully shifted to a more blitz-heavy approach — Colts ranked 2nd in blitz rate in Week 1. Uses 5-2 'Grizzly' fronts against heavy personnel and heavy nickel/dime against spread. His scheme in Indy is markedly more aggressive than his Cincinnati tenure.
defensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Extensive DB coaching background — developed Trey Hendrickson into an elite pass rusher (35 sacks in final two Bengals years), maximized Mike Hilton as a blitzing nickel (led NFL CBs in blitzes 2021-24), and developed Jessie Bates into an All-Pro safety. His secondary coaching roots mean he can develop coverage technique in DBs. However, his last two Cincinnati seasons showed difficulty developing young defenders quickly when veteran talent departed.

offense.scheme_family: RPO-based spread with run-game foundation (Steichen variant, Eagles/Chargers lineage)
offense.scheme_detail: Steichen's offense is built on establishing the run game as the foundation, then layering RPOs, play-action, bootlegs, and pre-snap motion on top to create easy reads for the QB. In Philadelphia, Steichen called RPOs on ~20% of plays (3rd in NFL). In Indianapolis, the offense has maintained that heavy RPO/play-action identity. The 2025 Colts offense was historically efficient, leading the NFL in points per game through the first half of the season. Steichen uses pre-snap motion as a diagnostic tool — the QB reads the defensive reaction to motion to determine coverage pre-snap, simplifying post-snap reads. The offense mixes inside zone, split zone, duo, and gap/power run concepts. The passing game emphasizes short-to-intermediate timing routes with occasional deep shots off play-action. Steichen adapts his scheme to his QB — with Daniel Jones in 2025, he leaned heavily into bootleg/rollout concepts and pre-snap diagnosis to maximize Jones' processing ability.
offense.run_scheme: Inside zone and split zone are primary concepts, with wham, duo, and gap/power mixed in. The Colts led the NFL in rush EPA in 2025. Jonathan Taylor rushed for 1,585 yards and 18 TDs. The OL is built to zone-block (Raimann, Nelson are excellent lateral movers) but versatile enough for power looks. Tyler Warren's ability to be used as a lead blocker in power formations or as a puller adds schematic flexibility. Pin-pull runs from 12-personnel have been particularly effective.
offense.pass_architecture: RPO-heavy with play-action and bootleg concepts as the foundation. Pre-snap motion is used to identify coverage before the snap, allowing the QB to attack the first read at a very high rate (Jones had a 97% first-read target rate in early 2025). Shot plays come off 12-personnel play-action, often targeting Alec Pierce deep or Tyler Warren on seam/crosser routes. Quick game (slants, hitches, screens) serves as the complementary layer. Not a heavy downfield attack — deep shots are selective and high-percentage, designed off run-action. Low sack rate (Colts allowed fewest sacks per game in NFL in early 2025) because of quick reads and RPO structure.
offense.personnel_tendencies: 11 personnel is the base (~65-70% usage), but 12 personnel is a significant and devastating wrinkle (~25% of plays in 2025). The Colts use 12 personnel to create mismatches — forcing defenses into 7-man boxes and then throwing play-action shots, or running power/pin-pull into lighter defensive fronts. Tyler Warren operates as a chess piece in 12 personnel, lining up in-line, flexed, in the slot, as a fullback, or detached wide. 21 personnel is used situationally. The Colts spread targets across many weapons (Pittman, Pierce, Downs, Warren, Taylor, Mitchell, Alie-Cox).
offense.motion_tempo: Heavy pre-snap motion, standard tempo. Motion is a diagnostic tool rather than a tempo weapon — Steichen uses it to give the QB a pre-snap coverage read. The 2025 offense was among the league leaders in pre-snap motion rate. Tempo is controlled and deliberate, not hurry-up. The offense is designed to be methodical and efficient, wearing defenses down rather than racing them.

defense.scheme_detail: Anarumo does not run a traditional 4-3 or 3-4 base. His defense is fundamentally multiple — he changes fronts, coverages, and pressure packages on a weekly and even play-by-play basis to attack opponent-specific weaknesses. In 2025 with the Colts, Anarumo shifted to a more blitz-heavy approach than his Cincinnati tenure, incorporating Tango pressures, Hot pressures, and Creeper pressures with Quarters (Palms) behind them. He heavily favors nickel (4-2-5) as the true base — the Colts had only 7 snaps with traditional 4-3 personnel in 2025. Against heavy personnel, he deploys a 5-2 'Grizzly' front rather than a traditional 4-3 base. He also uses significant dime (4-1-6) on passing downs. His philosophy: never give the QB the same picture twice.
defense.front_structure: Nickel (4-2-5) is the true base personnel grouping. Four down linemen (Buckner, Stewart inside; Latu, Paye/Ebukam on the edge) with two linebackers (Franklin, Bachie/Pratt) and five DBs. Against heavy offensive personnel (12, 13, 21), Anarumo deploys a 5-2 'Grizzly' front — five defensive linemen, two linebackers — rather than bringing in a third LB. The Colts used 159 Grizzly front snaps in 2025, allowing just 3.3 yards per carry. In dime, one LB comes off for a sixth DB. Anarumo explicitly stated his philosophy: 'The days of putting linebackers on tight ends is not ideal.'
defense.coverage_philosophy: True multiple coverage — Anarumo mixes zone and man roughly 57/43 (zone/man) but varies this significantly week-to-week based on the opponent. Cover 3 and Cover 2 are the primary zone shells. He has increasingly incorporated Quarters (Cover 4 Palms) behind pressure. Disguise is paramount — corners may appear in man but play zone, and vice versa. Anarumo was one of the first coordinators to champion safety rotation concepts ('two-becomes-one' rotations) as the base of his defense. He values length and tackling ability in CBs as much as pure speed. With the 2025 Colts, he mixed in aggressive man coverage looks on third downs — a significant shift from the passive zone of the previous Bradley regime.
defense.pressure_philosophy: Shifted dramatically in Indianapolis. In Cincinnati, Anarumo blitzed at a moderate rate (~26.5%). In Week 1 with the Colts, the blitz rate jumped to 41.1% (2nd in NFL). He uses five-man pressures with Quarters behind them (Tango pressures), simulated pressures where an LB or DB replaces a dropping DL, and creative safety/nickel blitzes. Mike Hilton led NFL CBs in blitzes under Anarumo in Cincinnati; Kenny Moore fills a similar blitzing nickel role in Indianapolis. Anarumo also proved he can rush just 3 and drop 8 when needed (AFC Championship vs. Chiefs). The key is unpredictability — the number, source, and timing of pressure changes constantly.
defense.sub_packages: Nickel (4-2-5) is the primary package — used on the vast majority of snaps. Dime (4-1-6) is used on obvious passing downs and was approximately 15% of snaps during Anarumo's peak Cincinnati years. The Colts acquired Cam Bynum (safety with Quarters-pressure experience from Minnesota) and Charvarius Ward (zone CB from SF) specifically to enable Anarumo's coverage flexibility. The nickel defender (Kenny Moore) is a true CB who can also blitz, tackle in the run game, and cover the slot. In dime, the sixth DB is typically a safety-type who can match up with TEs.

QB.scheme_role: The QB in Steichen's system is a pre-snap processor first and a thrower second. The offense uses heavy pre-snap motion to diagnose coverage before the snap, simplifying post-snap reads. The QB must read the defensive reaction to motion, determine the coverage, and attack the predetermined first read with accuracy and timing. RPO execution requires quick decision-making and a quick release — not necessarily elite arm strength or mobility. Play-action bootlegs ask the QB to operate outside the pocket and deliver throws on the move. Steichen has shown he can adapt to different QB styles (Rivers pocket passer, Hurts dual-threat, Jones pre-snap processor), but the consistent throughline is: the QB must make correct pre-snap reads and distribute the ball efficiently.

Arm Strength.reasoning: Steichen's offense doesn't require a cannon — the RPO/quick-game structure means most throws are short-to-intermediate. However, play-action shot plays to Alec Pierce and deep seams to Warren require enough arm to threaten vertically off run fakes.

Accuracy.reasoning: The offense is built on timing and efficiency — short windows on RPO throws, anticipation throws to the first read, and high completion rates that sustain drives. Daniel Jones' 97% first-read target rate in 2025 shows the system demands precise ball placement to the predetermined target.

Pocket Presence.reasoning: The Colts' OL is strong (lowest sack rate in NFL in early 2025), but RPOs require the QB to hold the ball momentarily while reading the LB — composure in the pocket under potential pressure is key. Bootleg concepts move the pocket but still require poise.

Mobility.reasoning: Higher than a pure West Coast system because Steichen's bootleg/rollout concepts require the QB to move outside the pocket and deliver throws on the move. Steichen's best offenses (Hurts in PHI, Jones in IND 2025) featured QBs who could scramble for controlled gains. Not pure speed — functional mobility to extend plays and execute designed movement.

Decision Making.reasoning: The single most critical cognitive trait. RPOs demand instant pre-snap diagnosis: read the LB, determine run or pass. Motion reads require identifying coverage before the snap. High first-read target rates mean the QB must be right the first time. Steichen's system is designed to simplify decisions, but the QB must process the simplified information correctly and quickly.

Leadership.reasoning: Steichen values a QB who commands the huddle and communicates with the OL — Jones' pre-snap audible on 4th down vs. Miami in Week 1 exemplifies this. Not the top priority but important for the run-heavy, physical identity Steichen builds.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: Overlaps with Decision Making but distinct — the ability to read the defense pre-snap and make adjustments (shifting receivers, calling audibles) is what unlocked Jones in this system. Steichen's motion-based offense provides the information; the QB must interpret it correctly.

QB.ideal_archetype: The ideal QB in Steichen's system is a poised, accurate, pre-snap processor who can efficiently distribute the ball to multiple targets. He doesn't need to be a gunslinger or an elite athlete — he needs to be right on his first read, accurate in timing windows, and mobile enough to execute bootleg/rollout concepts. Think Daniel Jones at his 2025 peak, or the Jalen Hurts model from the 2022 Eagles — a QB who uses motion and RPOs to simplify decisions, rarely turns the ball over, and can hurt defenses with his legs when the structure breaks down. Philip Rivers' late-career efficiency with Steichen in LA (pre-snap processing, quick release) is the pocket-passer variant of this archetype.

height.reasoning: Steichen has worked with QBs from 6-1 (Hurts) to 6-5 (Rivers/Jones). No rigid height requirement, but RPO execution benefits from seeing over the OL to read LBs pre-snap.

weight.reasoning: Mobility matters in this system — bootleg concepts and designed rollouts favor athletic builds. But durability in a run-heavy offense matters too.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Not a speed-based system, but Steichen's QBs benefit from being credible runners. Hurts (4.59), Richardson (4.43), and Jones (4.81 at pro day) represent the range. Functional mobility over pure speed.

QB.development_context: Steichen has proven he can develop QBs at different stages — he helped Hurts evolve from a limited passer into an MVP candidate, and he revived Daniel Jones' career from the scrap heap. Cooter adds QB development experience from working with Manning and Stafford. The staff can develop pre-snap processing and RPO execution. However, accuracy and arm talent are harder to teach. A prospect with strong processing skills and average arm strength fits better than the reverse — this staff will put the QB in positions to succeed schematically.
QB.scheme_fit_narrative: This offense is designed to make the QB's job as easy as possible. Motion tells you the coverage, RPOs give you a binary read, and the run game keeps defenses honest. We need a QB who can process that information correctly and deliver accurate throws to the first read. Arm talent is secondary to decision-making speed. Give us the smart, efficient processor — we'll scheme the explosive plays.

RB.scheme_role: The RB in this offense is THE featured weapon. Jonathan Taylor rushed for 1,585 yards and led the NFL in total and rushing touchdowns (18/20) in 2025. The scheme is built to funnel volume to the RB through inside zone, split zone, and gap/power concepts. The RB must be a workhorse who can carry 25+ times per game and grind out yards between the tackles. Pass-catching is secondary but valuable — Taylor catches 30-40 balls per year out of the backfield. The RB is also a critical part of RPO execution, as the run threat is what freezes LBs and makes the RPO passing options viable.

Vision.reasoning: Inside zone and split zone require the RB to read blocks in real time and find cutback lanes. Taylor's vision is what makes the Colts' relatively simple run scheme devastating — he reads the zone blocks, finds the crease, and accelerates through it.

Contact Balance.reasoning: Steichen's run-to-win philosophy means the RB takes a heavy workload and faces stacked boxes late in games. The ability to stay on feet through contact and fall forward for extra yards is critical to the ball-control identity.

Power.reasoning: Gap/power concepts from 12-personnel require the RB to run behind lead blockers and finish through arm tackles at the second level. The Colts mix in pin-pull and duo runs that create downhill lanes — the RB must hit them with authority.

Elusiveness.reasoning: Zone scheme creates space but the RB must make the first defender miss to turn 4-yard runs into 8-yard runs. Not the primary trait — vision and power come first — but elusiveness at the second level creates explosive plays.

Speed.reasoning: Taylor's home-run speed is a bonus, not a requirement. The scheme is designed to create lanes through blocking, not to win with pure speed in space. However, breakaway speed turns good runs into touchdowns and forces safeties to respect the deep run threat.

Pass Catching.reasoning: The RB catches balls out of the backfield on RPO bubbles, swing routes, and check-downs. Taylor had 8 targets through 3 weeks in 2025. Not a primary receiving role but the RB must be reliable as a release valve.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Play-action and bootleg concepts require the RB to occasionally stay in and protect. The offense's low sack rate partly depends on the RB chipping or picking up delayed blitzers.

RB.ideal_archetype: A high-volume, between-the-tackles workhorse with elite vision in zone schemes, the power to finish runs through contact, and enough speed to break long runs. Think Jonathan Taylor — a complete back who can carry the ball 300+ times in a season, read zone blocking at an elite level, contribute in the passing game, and be the offense's primary engine. Derrick Henry's power profile or Nick Chubb's vision/power combo also fit, though the ideal also includes receiving capability.

height.reasoning: Workhorse backs need to be big enough to absorb a heavy workload. Taylor is 5-10, 226 — the prototype for this system.

weight.reasoning: Power and durability are critical for a 300-carry back. The Colts' RB must run behind the OL into stacked boxes, requiring mass to finish through tackles.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Breakaway speed is a plus in zone schemes — the ability to turn the corner on outside zone or accelerate through creases. Not a pure speed requirement but enough burst to create explosives.

RB.development_context: Cooter spent time as a Jets RB coach and worked with Le'Veon Bell and Frank Gore — he understands RB development. RB coach DeAndre Smith handles day-to-day development. The staff can teach zone-reading technique and pass protection. What they need to arrive ready: vision and instincts in zone schemes. Those are largely innate traits.
RB.scheme_fit_narrative: This is a bell-cow role. We need a back who can be the engine of the offense — 25+ carries per game, reading zone blocks, finishing through contact, and being reliable enough to carry the offense in the fourth quarter when we're grinding out wins. Speed is a bonus, but vision and power are non-negotiable.

WR.scheme_role: WRs in Steichen's offense play varied roles depending on alignment. The X receiver (Pittman/Pierce) must be able to win contested catches on deep play-action shots and be reliable on intermediate routes. The slot (Downs) works the middle of the field on quick-game and RPO concepts. The secondary outside receiver (Mitchell) provides versatility. The offense distributes targets broadly — no WR is force-fed. WRs must be able to win individually because the scheme often isolates them in one-on-one matchups created by personnel groupings and motion. In 12-personnel, WRs get single coverage — they must capitalize.

Route Running.reasoning: The timing-based passing game requires WRs to run precise routes and be in the right spot at the right time. RPO passing options are short-window throws — the WR must create separation at the break quickly.

Separation.reasoning: 12-personnel usage creates single-coverage matchups — WRs must win these one-on-one situations. The offense creates the opportunity; the WR must capitalize through separation skills.

Hands.reasoning: Reliable hands are critical in a system where the QB is throwing to his first read at a high rate. Drops kill the efficiency model. The offense can't afford unreliable targets.

YAC Ability.reasoning: Steichen's RPO and quick-game concepts deliver the ball early — the WR must create yards after the catch. Screen plays, slants, and shallow crossers all depend on YAC. Josh Downs' slot role is particularly YAC-dependent.

Speed.reasoning: Play-action shots to Alec Pierce (who led the NFL in yards per reception in 2025) require a vertical threat. Speed opens up the intermediate game underneath. The offense needs at least one WR who can take the top off the defense to make RPOs work.

Contested Catches.reasoning: Pierce's role is largely built on winning contested catches on deep balls. Pittman's role also requires catching in traffic over the middle. The offense isolates WRs in 1-on-1 situations where contested catch ability matters.

Release Package.reasoning: Less critical than in a pure man-coverage beater scheme because the offense uses motion and formation to create leverage advantages pre-snap. But clean releases against press are still valuable, especially for the X receiver.

WR.ideal_archetype: The ideal outside WR is a big, fast target who can win deep on play-action shots and be reliable on intermediate routes — think Alec Pierce's role (6-3, 4.41 speed, 1,003 receiving yards in 2025). The ideal slot WR is a quick, shifty separator who excels after the catch — think Josh Downs or Cole Beasley. Steichen doesn't need the WR1 to be a target hog; he needs WRs who can win their individual matchups when the scheme creates them.

height_outside.reasoning: Play-action deep shots often result in contested situations — size helps win those. Pierce (6-3) and Pittman (6-4) set the template.

height_slot.reasoning: Slot receivers need quickness and agility over size in this system. Downs (5-9) is the template.

40-yard dash.reasoning: The deep threat is essential for the play-action game to work. At least one outside WR must have legitimate deep speed to stretch defenses vertically.

WR.development_context: WR coach Reggie Wayne (Hall of Fame caliber) brings elite route-running development expertise. The staff can develop route technique and contested-catch skills. Steichen has shown he can elevate previously overlooked WRs (Pierce went from 'bust' to 1,000-yard receiver). What must arrive ready: speed and ball skills. Those are the traits that can't be coached.
WR.scheme_fit_narrative: We need WRs who can win when isolated in single coverage. The scheme creates favorable matchups through 12-personnel and motion — the WR has to finish the play. Give us a vertical threat who can take the top off on play-action, a reliable possession receiver, and a shifty slot who can create after the catch. This offense spreads the ball around, so ego isn't welcome — production comes from winning your individual matchup when the scheme sets it up.

TE.scheme_role: The TE is the most important non-QB offensive position in this scheme. Tyler Warren (No. 14 overall pick, 2025) set a franchise rookie record with 76 receptions and 817 receiving yards while playing 899 of 1,075 offensive snaps. The TE in this offense is a true chess piece — he lines up in-line, flexed to the slot, detached wide, as a fullback, and as a puller in power schemes. He must block in the run game (critical for the inside zone/split zone foundation), run a full route tree (seam routes, crossers, flats off play-action), and contribute as a receiver in the RPO game. The 12-personnel package is a devastating weapon specifically because the TE creates mismatches — forcing defenses to choose between defending the run (with a blocker) and the pass (with a receiving threat). This role demands a complete TE.

Receiving.reasoning: Warren's 76 receptions and 817 yards as a rookie show this role is heavily receiving-oriented. The TE is the primary weapon in 12-personnel passing concepts, targeted in the seam, on crossers off play-action, and on screens. The ability to catch in traffic and be a reliable target is paramount.

Route Running.reasoning: Warren ran a full route tree as a rookie — seam routes, shallow crossers, corner routes, and out-breaking routes. The TE must run routes with the precision of a WR when flexed out, and the scheme uses him all over the formation, requiring diverse route-running ability.

Blocking.reasoning: The run game is the foundation, and the TE must be an effective in-line blocker on inside zone, split zone, and wham concepts. Warren's ability to crack-block the backside DE on split zone AND be used as a puller in power is what makes 12-personnel work. A TE who can't block in this system forces the offense out of 12-personnel, removing a major weapon.

Athleticism.reasoning: The chess-piece usage requires a TE who can line up everywhere — in-line, slot, wing, fullback, detached. That requires the athletic range to block DEs, run with LBs in coverage, and create separation against safeties. Warren's versatility (screen plays, puller, seam routes) is built on athleticism.

Hands.reasoning: High target volume (7+ targets per game for Warren) means the TE must be a reliable catcher. Play-action throws to the TE are often in tight windows over the middle — reliable hands are critical.

Speed.reasoning: The TE doesn't need WR speed, but enough speed to stretch seams, run screens for YAC, and create mismatches against slower LBs. Warren's speed on screen plays in 2025 generated explosive plays.

TE.ideal_archetype: The ideal TE is Tyler Warren himself — a complete chess piece who can block in-line on zone runs, run a full route tree as a receiver, line up anywhere from fullback to split wide, and produce as a high-volume target. Think Dallas Goedert (Steichen's TE in Philadelphia), Travis Kelce (chess-piece usage), or George Kittle (blocking + receiving). The key differentiator is versatility — this scheme demands a TE who is equally credible as a blocker and a receiver, not one or the other.

height.reasoning: Size matters for in-line blocking against DEs and for contested catches in the red zone. Warren is 6-6, 260 — the prototype.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to hold up as an in-line blocker against NFL DEs but athletic enough to run routes. The dual demands require a specific physical profile.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Enough speed to threaten seams and create mismatches against LBs. Not WR speed but legitimate athleticism for a 250+ pound player.

TE.development_context: TE coach Tom Manning has experience developing TEs in this scheme. Steichen's track record with Goedert in Philadelphia shows he can maximize a versatile TE. The staff can develop route-running technique and receiving skills. What must arrive ready: blocking willingness and technique. Steichen specifically praised Warren's ability to handle both blocking and passing schemes — a TE who arrives as a pure receiver would need significant blocking development.
TE.scheme_fit_narrative: The TE is the fulcrum of our offense. He has to be able to line up anywhere, block anyone, and catch everything. We're not looking for a glorified WR or a sixth OL — we need a complete player who can do both at a high level. If we can trust the TE to block on 1st and 2nd down and then split him out on 3rd down, the defense can never get comfortable. That's the Warren model, and it's what makes our 12-personnel package the most dangerous weapon in football.

OT.scheme_role: The OTs anchor a zone-blocking scheme that also incorporates power and gap concepts. The LT (Raimann) is the primary pass protector and a key run-blocker on zone runs to the left — Steichen specifically ran more plays in Raimann's direction in 2023. The RT (Smith) faces more tight splits and in-line blocking assignments. Both tackles must be able to zone-step and reach-block in the run game while holding up in pass protection against speed rushers. The Colts' low sack rate (best in NFL early in 2025) makes pass protection a proven strength of this OL unit.

Pass Protection.reasoning: The RPO-based passing game requires the QB to hold the ball momentarily while reading LBs. If the OTs can't protect, the entire offensive structure collapses. The Colts' historically low sack rate is a coaching staff priority.

Run Blocking.reasoning: Inside zone and split zone are the foundation — OTs must zone-step, reach-block, and climb to the second level. The run game is not an afterthought; it's the identity. Taylor's 1,585 yards depend on OT run-blocking.

Footwork.reasoning: Zone blocking demands quick, precise footwork — lateral steps to reach-block on zone runs, kick-slides against speed rushers in pass protection. The Colts mix zone and power, requiring versatile footwork.

Anchor.reasoning: Important for pass protection against bull-rush specialists, but the quick-passing RPO game mitigates the need for elite anchor — the ball comes out fast.

Athleticism.reasoning: Zone blocking and pin-pull schemes demand athletic OTs who can move laterally and get to the second level. Raimann's athleticism (one of the best run-block win rates in the NFL) is the template.

Strength.reasoning: Base strength matters for sustaining blocks but is secondary to movement skills in this zone scheme. Power concepts require some strength to execute drive blocks but the scheme doesn't rely on brute force as its primary weapon.

OT.ideal_archetype: An athletic, quick-footed zone blocker who can also anchor in pass protection. Think Bernhard Raimann — a former receiver who converted to OT and brings exceptional lateral movement and foot speed to zone blocking while developing as a pass protector. The ideal OT in this system moves well, processes quickly, and can execute both zone and power assignments. Lane Johnson (Steichen's RT in Philadelphia) is the gold standard — elite athlete, strong pass protector, excellent in the zone run game.

height.reasoning: Standard NFL OT requirements. The zone scheme doesn't demand outlier height but adequate length for pass protection.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to anchor against power but light enough to move laterally. Zone blockers are typically lighter than pure power blockers.

short_shuttle.reasoning: Zone blocking requires lateral agility — the ability to reach-block and get to the second level. Short shuttle is a key indicator of zone-blocking ability.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Athletic OTs who can pull, reach-block, and climb are valued in this scheme. Raimann's athletic profile is the template.

OT.development_context: OL coach Tony Sparano Jr. has been excellent at developing the Colts' OL — Raimann has improved every year under his coaching. The staff can develop technique, particularly in zone-blocking footwork. What must arrive ready: basic athletic ability and football IQ. The staff has successfully developed mid-round picks (Raimann was a 3rd rounder) into starters.
OT.scheme_fit_narrative: Our OL has to move. Zone blocking is the foundation, and we need tackles who can reach-block, climb to the second level, and sustain blocks on the move. But they also have to protect — our low sack rate is a point of pride. Give us the athletic OT who can do both. The raw mauler who can't move his feet won't work here.

IOL.scheme_role: The IOL anchors the most important part of the Colts' offense — the run game. Quenton Nelson (6x All-Pro) is the centerpiece, providing elite run-blocking that drives the entire offensive identity. The center (Bortolini) must handle Steichen's RPO snap mechanics and make protection calls. The guards must zone-step, pull on power/pin-pull concepts, and sustain drive blocks on duo runs. The 2025 Colts finished top 10 in rushing for the second straight year. IOL versatility is increasingly important — Goncalves and Bortolini were 2024 draft picks who started as sophomores in 2025.

Pass Protection.reasoning: The RPO structure means quick-release passes, reducing the need for sustained pass protection. But the IOL must still handle interior pressure and stunts competently. The Colts' low sack rate depends on interior protection.

Run Blocking.reasoning: THE defining trait. The Colts' identity is the run game, and the IOL must dominate at the point of attack. Inside zone, duo, and gap/power all require elite interior run blocking. Nelson's run-blocking is the single most impactful non-QB trait on this offense.

Pulling.reasoning: Pin-pull runs from 12-personnel have been a devastating wrinkle in 2025. Guards must be athletic enough to pull and lead on power/counter concepts. Nelson's pulling ability is a scheme weapon.

Strength.reasoning: Duo runs require double-teams that displace DTs at the point of attack. The IOL must have the base strength to win the line of scrimmage consistently in a run-heavy scheme.

Anchor.reasoning: Important but secondary to run-blocking — the quick-passing game reduces time the IOL must hold pass-pro sets. Still must handle bull rushes from interior DTs.

Versatility.reasoning: The Colts have asked IOL to play multiple positions (Pinter as backup C, Tucker as swing guard). Versatility provides roster flexibility. The diverse run scheme (zone, power, duo, wham) requires IOL who can execute different blocking assignments.

IOL.ideal_archetype: Quenton Nelson IS the archetype — a mauling, athletic guard who dominates in the run game, pulls with devastating effect, and is reliable in pass protection. For the center, think a smart, athletic pivot who can handle RPO snap mechanics and make protection calls quickly. The ideal IOL in this scheme is a physical road-grader who can also move his feet in zone concepts. Think Nelson, Joel Bitonio, or Tyler Linderbaum (athletic, smart, run-game dominant).

height_guard.reasoning: Standard guard size. The scheme doesn't demand outlier height but enough frame to sustain blocks.

weight_guard.reasoning: Mass matters for point-of-attack power in the run game. Nelson at 330 is the upper end. Must be heavy enough to dominate at the LOS.

short_shuttle.reasoning: Zone-blocking and pulling require lateral agility. A stiff guard who can't move hurts the zone game.

bench_press.reasoning: Upper body strength correlates with sustaining blocks in the run game, which is the primary demand of this position.

IOL.development_context: Sparano Jr.'s development of Bortolini and Goncalves from Day 2 picks into starters shows the staff can develop IOL. The staff successfully transitioned from Ryan Kelly to Bortolini at center without major dropoff. Zone-blocking technique and pulling can be developed. What must arrive: raw power and football IQ. The staff can't teach strength or processing speed.
IOL.scheme_fit_narrative: The interior line is where games are won in this offense. We need maulers who can move — guys who dominate in the run game, execute zone steps, pull on power, and hold up against interior pass rush. Quenton Nelson is the standard. If you can block like Nelson, you're a Colt.

EDGE.scheme_role: The EDGE in Anarumo's system is primarily a pass rusher from a 4-point or 3-point stance in a 4-man front. Laiatu Latu (8.5 sacks, 3 INTs in 2025) and Kwity Paye/Samson Ebukam share snaps. Anarumo's philosophy is clear: 'Corners and rushers. You have to be able to affect the quarterback.' The EDGE must generate pressure as part of a 4-man rush to allow Anarumo to drop 7 or 8 into coverage. In the Grizzly (5-2) front, a fifth DL is added, meaning EDGEs control C-gaps. Anarumo also asks EDGEs to occasionally drop into zone coverage on simulated pressures, and Latu's 3 INTs in 2025 show the value of EDGE players who can be versatile in the passing game. The scheme is position-flexible — Anarumo stated the 3-4/4-3 distinction doesn't matter, what matters is affecting the QB.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Anarumo's entire defensive philosophy depends on generating pressure to support his complex coverage schemes. He explicitly stated 'Corners and rushers' are the most important positions. The EDGE must be a legitimate pass-rush threat to make the drop-8 and complex blitz packages work — the front four generating pressure is what allows Anarumo to play the coverage games he loves.

Bend.reasoning: Anarumo values speed-to-power rushers who can win around the edge. Trey Hendrickson (35 sacks in 2 years under Anarumo) won with explosive bend around the corner. The Colts' EDGEs need to convert speed into sacks.

First Step.reasoning: Anarumo's pressure scheme benefits from quick-twitch EDGEs who can threaten immediately off the snap. The 5-man pressures with Quarters behind them require at least one EDGE to win quickly to make the coverage work before the QB has time to process.

Power.reasoning: A secondary rush move — EDGEs need a counter when bend doesn't work. But this isn't a power-rush-first scheme. Hendrickson won primarily with speed and technique, not bull-rushing.

Motor.reasoning: Anarumo's rotating fronts and heavy blitz packages mean the EDGE may not always be the primary rusher on every snap. High motor matters for clean-up plays, chasing QBs flushed from the pocket, and contributing on run downs.

Run Defense.reasoning: In the Grizzly (5-2) front, EDGEs are C-gap players who must control the edge in the run game. The 5-2 held opponents to 3.3 YPC in 2025. EDGEs must set the edge and not get washed down. This is elevated compared to a pure 4-3 system because of the 5-2 usage.

EDGE.ideal_archetype: A speed-based pass rusher with the versatility to drop into coverage occasionally and the discipline to defend the run in the 5-2 front. Think Trey Hendrickson (Anarumo's greatest EDGE development success — explosive first step, relentless motor, 17.5 sacks in 2024) or Laiatu Latu (8.5 sacks, 3 INTs showing scheme versatility). Not a pure bull-rush specialist — the scheme values explosiveness and the ability to affect the QB with speed around the corner.

height.reasoning: Anarumo said edge rushers 'come in all shapes and sizes.' He's not locked into a specific frame. What matters is production.

weight.reasoning: Anarumo noted bigger guys are more durable, but speed rushers are 'the commodity.' Not a heavy end scheme — explosive athletes preferred over big bodies.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Speed off the edge is paramount. Hendrickson and Latu both win with explosive first steps and speed around the corner. This scheme creates favorable rush lanes for athletes who can close quickly.

EDGE.development_context: Anarumo developed Hendrickson from a mid-level starter into an All-Pro pass rusher. Marion Hobby (new DL coach, hired from Cincinnati) worked directly with Hendrickson during those peak years. The staff can develop pass-rush technique and counter moves. Latu improved from 4 sacks as a rookie to 8.5 in year two. What must arrive: explosive athletic traits. You can teach hand technique but not first-step quickness.
EDGE.scheme_fit_narrative: We need guys who can affect the quarterback. Period. Anarumo's defense is built on generating pressure with the front four so he can play creative coverage behind it. Give us the explosive, twitchy pass rusher who can win one-on-one. If he can also set the edge in the run game and occasionally drop into coverage, that's the complete package. But the first question is always: can he rush the passer?

DL.scheme_role: Interior DL (Buckner, Stewart) are the foundation of Anarumo's front. Buckner is a pass-rushing 3-tech who generates interior pressure. Stewart is a massive nose tackle who commands double teams and anchors the run defense. In the Grizzly (5-2) front, the DL must occupy blockers to free up LBs. In nickel, the DL must generate interior pressure while being stout against the run. Anarumo's scheme asks interior DL to play multiple alignments — 1-tech, 3-tech, and occasionally shading to different gaps based on the front call. The versatility to line up in different spots and execute different gap responsibilities is critical.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Interior pressure is the most valuable pressure in football. Buckner is a proven interior pass rusher, and Anarumo's scheme benefits enormously from a DT who can generate pressure without blitzing. This allows Anarumo to drop LBs and DBs into coverage.

Run Defense.reasoning: The Grizzly (5-2) front's success (3.3 YPC allowed in 2025) depends on interior DL holding the point of attack. Stewart's ability to command double teams is what makes the 5-2 work. Against heavy personnel, run defense is the DL's primary responsibility.

First Step.reasoning: Penetration ability matters for the 3-tech role (Buckner) but less so for the nose (Stewart). The scheme asks different things of different DL spots.

Hand Usage.reasoning: Anarumo's DL must defeat blocks at the point of attack and disengage to make plays. In a multiple front with varied gap assignments, hands must be active and quick to get off blocks.

Motor.reasoning: DL rotation is deep (Buckner, Stewart, Gallimore, Johnson II, Adebawore, etc.) which manages snap counts. Motor still matters for effort plays, but the rotation mitigates the need for 70-snap DTs.

Strength.reasoning: Holding the point of attack in the 5-2 is a strength-dependent task. The nose must command double teams. The 3-tech must hold ground against single blocks while still being able to penetrate gaps.

DL.ideal_archetype: Two distinct archetypes needed: (1) The penetrating 3-tech (Buckner model) — athletic, explosive DT who can rush the passer from the interior while being disciplined against the run. (2) The anchoring nose (Stewart model) — massive, immovable object who commands double teams and controls the A-gaps. Anarumo's multiple front needs both types. Think Chris Jones (interior pass rusher) and Vita Vea (run-stuffing nose) as the ideal pairing.

height_3tech.reasoning: Length helps with hand placement and disruption. Buckner at 6-7 is the upper end but length is an advantage in this scheme.

weight_3tech.reasoning: Must be explosive enough to rush the passer while heavy enough to hold ground on run downs.

weight_nose.reasoning: The nose in the 5-2 must be a space-eater who commands attention. Mass is critical.

bench_press.reasoning: Upper body strength is directly relevant to hand fighting and anchoring at the point of attack.

DL.development_context: Marion Hobby (new DL coach from Cincinnati) worked with Hendrickson and the Bengals' DL during their best seasons. Charlie Partridge (retained DL coach) developed Adebawore and Johnson II. The staff has developed mid-round DL picks into contributors. What must arrive: size and explosiveness. Technique can be taught, but physical traits cannot.
DL.scheme_fit_narrative: We need two types of interior DL. The 3-tech has to get to the QB from the inside — that's the most valuable pressure in football. The nose has to hold the fort and eat up blocks so our LBs can run free. If a DT can do both — rush and run-stuff — that's the unicorn. But we'll take the specialist who does one thing at an elite level.

LB.scheme_role: Anarumo's system is explicitly a two-linebacker defense. Chris Ballard stated the scheme will feature 'a MIKE and a WILL' rather than the traditional MIKE/WILL/SAM. This means the two LBs on the field carry enormous responsibility — they must process coverages, fill run gaps, and occasionally rush the passer. Zaire Franklin (173 tackles, 11 TFL, 5 forced fumbles in 2024) is the MIKE — the defensive QB who makes calls and tackles everything. The WILL is more of an athlete/coverage type. Because Anarumo uses nickel as his base, LBs must be comfortable playing with 5 DBs behind them. In the Grizzly (5-2) front, LBs have clean run-fits because the 5 DL control the line — the LBs scrape and tackle.

Tackling.reasoning: With only two LBs on the field, both must be sure tacklers. Franklin's league-leading 173 tackles in 2024 show the tackling volume demanded of the MIKE. Missed tackles with only 2 LBs are catastrophic.

Coverage.reasoning: Anarumo explicitly stated 'the days of putting linebackers on tight ends is not ideal' and prefers DBs in that role — but LBs must still handle zone drops effectively. In the Grizzly front vs. heavy personnel, LBs may have to carry TEs or RBs in coverage. Zone drop discipline and awareness are critical.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Anarumo uses creative blitz packages including delayed LB rushes and A-gap blitzes. LBs occasionally rush on Tango/Creeper pressures. Not a primary responsibility but the ability to rush adds value to the pressure package.

Instincts.reasoning: The MIKE LB is the defensive QB — he must diagnose formations, identify the run/pass key, and get the defense aligned. Franklin's instincts are what allow him to rack up tackles and create turnovers (5 forced fumbles). In a scheme that changes weekly, instincts are what allow LBs to play fast within unfamiliar structures.

Athleticism.reasoning: Two-LB defenses need athletic LBs who can range sideline-to-sideline. The Grizzly front creates clean run-fits, but LBs must get there quickly. Nickel defense requires LBs who can move in space.

Range.reasoning: With only 2 LBs, each must cover more ground. Range to the sideline on zone drops and range against the run when the 5 DL funnel everything outside is important.

Block Shedding.reasoning: The 5-2 front is designed to keep OL off LBs — the DL occupy blockers so LBs run free. Block shedding is less critical than in a traditional 4-3 where LBs take on blocks regularly.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: The MIKE must set the defense in a scheme that changes dramatically week-to-week. Anarumo's weekly game-planning puts a premium on LBs who can absorb new information and execute unfamiliar assignments quickly.

LB.ideal_archetype: The ideal MIKE is Zaire Franklin — a sideline-to-sideline tackling machine who can set the defense, make calls, and be reliable in every situation. He doesn't need to be an elite athlete; he needs to be a high-IQ, high-volume tackler who rarely misses. The ideal WILL is more athletic — a coverage/run hybrid who can handle TEs in man and rush on blitz packages. Think Logan Wilson (Anarumo's LB in Cincinnati) — smart, athletic, productive in coverage and as a blitzer.

height.reasoning: Standard LB size. No outlier requirements — Anarumo values instincts and processing over physical measurables at LB.

weight.reasoning: Must be physical enough to take on interior linemen in the run game but athletic enough to cover in space.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Range and sideline-to-sideline ability matter in a 2-LB defense. Athletic LBs who can cover ground are preferred.

LB.development_context: LB coach James Bettcher (former Cardinals and Giants DC) has extensive experience developing LBs. He followed Anarumo from Cincinnati where he coached Logan Wilson and Germaine Pratt. The staff can develop tackling technique, run-fit discipline, and zone drop skills. What must arrive: football IQ and processing ability. In a scheme that changes weekly, LBs who can't learn quickly will be left behind.
LB.scheme_fit_narrative: We only play two LBs, so they better be good. The MIKE is the defensive quarterback — he sets the defense and tackles everything. The WILL needs to be athletic enough to cover and rush. We're not asking our LBs to take on blocks like a traditional 4-3 — our front handles that. What we need is intelligence, reliability, and range.

CB.scheme_role: CBs in Anarumo's defense must be versatile enough to play both man and zone coverage convincingly — this is the essence of his disguise-heavy scheme. The outside CBs (Ward, Jones, Howard, Sauce Gardner acquired mid-2025) must play press-man on third downs and zone (Cover 3, Cover 2, Quarters) on early downs, and must be able to make it look like one when playing the other. The nickel CB (Kenny Moore) has a distinctive role — he's a blitzing, tackling slot defender who led NFL CBs in blitzes under Anarumo in Cincinnati (via Mike Hilton). Anarumo explicitly values length, speed, change of direction, and tackling in CBs. His background as a longtime DB coach means he prioritizes secondary play above all.

Man Coverage.reasoning: Anarumo's Bengals played man coverage on 43.3% of dropbacks — significantly higher than the previous Bradley regime's soft zone. The Colts mixed in aggressive man coverage in 2025. CBs must be able to match up 1-on-1 in man, especially on third downs.

Ball Skills.reasoning: Anarumo's defense creates turnovers — the 2024 Bengals finished top-10 in INTs and passes defensed. The Colts had the most takeaways in Week 1 of 2025 (3). Ball skills translate to Anarumo's disguise concepts because when the QB throws into what he thinks is zone but is actually man, the CB must capitalize.

Zone Coverage.reasoning: Zone is still the majority of coverage calls (56.7% zone in Cincinnati). CBs must play Cover 3 flats, Cover 2 halves, and Quarters with discipline and awareness. The ability to disguise zone as man (and vice versa) is what makes the coverage scheme work.

Speed.reasoning: Anarumo stated 'speed, change of direction, all that stuff is paramount.' CBs must be able to recover when beaten and have the long speed to match vertical threats. The AFC South features fast receivers.

Press.reasoning: Anarumo uses aggressive press-man looks, especially on third downs. The 2025 preseason showed the Colts 'mixing in aggressive man coverage looks and suffocating the sticks.' Press technique is essential for the man coverage component.

Nickel.reasoning: Kenny Moore's nickel role is THE signature defensive position in this scheme. The nickel must cover slots, blitz, tackle in the run game, and occasionally play outside. Anarumo's system runs nickel as the base, making this role critical. This weight applies primarily to nickel-type CBs.

CB.ideal_archetype: The outside CB is a long, physical corner who can play press-man and also has the IQ to execute zone concepts and disguise coverage. Think Charvarius Ward — a zone corner who can play man when needed, with the length (6-0+) and physicality Anarumo values. Or Sauce Gardner — an elite man-coverage CB who is also disciplined in zone. The nickel is Kenny Moore — a tough, versatile slot defender who blitzes, tackles, and covers. Anarumo values CBs who can 'make it look like they're in man but they're playing zone' — football IQ is as important as physical traits.

height_outside.reasoning: Anarumo explicitly stated 'length really allows you to be a good corner in this league.' He signed Ward (6-1), Gardner (6-3), and Howard (6-1). Length is strongly preferred.

height_nickel.reasoning: Nickel corners can be smaller — Moore is 5-9. Quickness and toughness matter more than size in the slot.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Speed is 'paramount' per Anarumo. Recovery speed in man coverage and closing speed in zone both require legitimate long speed.

arm_length_outside.reasoning: Length correlates with press ability and ball disruption at the catch point — directly tied to Anarumo's emphasis on long, physical outside corners.

CB.development_context: Anarumo has an extensive background coaching DBs (Purdue, Miami, NYG) — it's his core competency. Jerome Henderson (DBs coach) and Chris Hewitt (pass game coordinator) add deep secondary development experience. This staff can develop coverage technique, press mechanics, and zone reads. What must arrive: speed and change of direction. Those are physical traits that can't be coached. Anarumo can teach a fast, athletic CB to play both man and zone.
CB.scheme_fit_narrative: We need corners who can do both — man and zone, press and off, outside and slot. Our scheme changes every week, and the CBs have to disguise what they're doing. Length and speed are the starting points — you can't fake those. But what separates the good from the great in this defense is IQ: the ability to make it look like man when you're playing zone, and vice versa. We want smart, physical, versatile corners who can tackle, too.

S.scheme_role: Safeties are the most schematically complex position in Anarumo's defense. He pioneered 'two-becomes-one' safety rotations — pre-snap, both safeties may show a two-high look, but one rotates down post-snap into a single-high or box role. The free safety (Bynum) must have the range to play single-high in Cover 1/Cover 3 after rotating from a two-high shell. The strong safety (Cross) must be able to play in the box against the run and match TEs in coverage. Anarumo specifically acquired Bynum from Minnesota because of his experience in Quarters-based pressures. Safety versatility is THE defining requirement — the ability to play multiple roles within a single game, or even a single drive.

Man Coverage.reasoning: Safeties in Anarumo's system must match TEs and RBs in man coverage — a critical role when the scheme calls for man looks. Bynum's versatility in coverage was a key reason for his signing.

Range.reasoning: The safety rotation scheme requires one safety to cover deep halves or the deep middle after rotating from a two-high pre-snap look. This demands elite range — the ability to get from the hash to the boundary quickly. Bynum's 4 INTs in 2025 reflect his range and ball-hawking ability.

Ball Skills.reasoning: Anarumo's coverage disguises create opportunities for safeties to jump routes when QBs misread coverage. Turnovers are a priority — the Colts targeted creating takeaways and Bynum delivered. Ball skills are what convert good coverage into game-changing plays.

Tackling.reasoning: Anarumo listed tackling in 'the top three' of traits for defensive backs. With only 2 LBs on the field, safeties must be reliable tacklers — especially the box safety who functions almost as a third LB against the run. Missed tackles at safety are often touchdowns.

Speed.reasoning: Range and recovery speed are directly tied to physical speed. The safety rotation scheme is impossible to execute without legitimate speed — the post-snap rotation must be fast enough to get into position before the route develops.

Nickel.reasoning: In dime packages, safeties may play hybrid roles covering TEs or playing in the slot. Anarumo has historically used safety/LB hybrids (Jessie Bates as a deep-middle/box hybrid, Vonn Bell as a box safety who could cover). The ability to play in the slot or match up with TEs adds scheme flexibility.

S.ideal_archetype: The free safety is Jessie Bates III / Cam Bynum — a rangy, ball-hawking centerfielder who can play deep middle, rotate from two-high to single-high post-snap, and make plays on the ball. The strong safety is Vonn Bell / Nick Cross — a physical, box-capable safety who can tackle in the run game, blitz on pressure packages, and cover TEs in man. Both must be smart enough to handle Anarumo's complex rotation scheme and disciplined enough to execute disguise assignments.

height.reasoning: Standard safety size. The free safety needs enough length to contest catches at the catch point; the strong safety needs enough size to tackle in the box.

weight.reasoning: Box safeties need to be physical enough to play near the LOS. Deep safeties can be lighter but must be fast.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Range is the defining trait for the free safety — speed directly translates to range. The rotation scheme demands safeties who can cover ground quickly post-snap.

S.development_context: Anarumo's entire coaching career started in the secondary — this is his area of greatest developmental expertise. Chris Hewitt (pass game coordinator) spent 13 years developing DBs in Baltimore. The staff can develop coverage technique, zone reads, and safety rotation execution. What must arrive: athleticism and ball skills. The staff can teach disguise and rotation concepts, but they can't teach range.
S.scheme_fit_narrative: The safety position is the engine of our coverage scheme. We need a deep safety who can play centerfield and a box safety who can play like a third linebacker. Both have to be smart enough to handle weekly disguise packages and athletic enough to rotate from one role to another in the blink of an eye. Ball skills are huge — we want turnovers. Tackling is non-negotiable. Give us the versatile, smart, athletic safety who can do it all.

scheme_draft_intelligence:
The Colts' 2025 coaching staff creates a unique set of draft priorities shaped by Shane Steichen's run-first, RPO-based offense and Lou Anarumo's multiple, disguise-heavy defense. The biggest value differential comes at tight end — Steichen's 12-personnel package is a devastating weapon, and any TE who can block AND receive becomes exponentially more valuable to Indianapolis than to teams running spread-only schemes. Tyler Warren's rookie production (76 catches, 817 yards, 899 snaps) shows the scheme's appetite for chess-piece TEs. A second TE who can complement Warren would further elevate the 12-personnel weapon. Similarly, interior offensive linemen who can zone-block AND pull are disproportionately valuable — the Colts' run identity depends on IOL who move well.

Defensively, Anarumo's scheme creates particular value for versatile defensive backs. A corner who can play man AND zone, or a safety who can play deep AND in the box, is far more valuable to Indianapolis than to a defense that plays exclusively Cover 3. The week-to-week schematic flexibility means Anarumo needs players who can execute multiple assignments rather than specialists who do one thing well. His emphasis on nickel/dime defense (the Colts had only 7 snaps with a traditional 4-3 in 2025) also means that a third cornerback or a dime safety is effectively a starter, not a role player — creating hidden value in versatile secondary prospects that other teams might view as Day 2 or 3 picks.

Edge rusher is the position where the Colts' scheme creates the most aggressive valuation. Anarumo explicitly identified 'corners and rushers' as his priorities, and his development track record (Hendrickson from mid-level to All-Pro) suggests he can maximize raw pass-rush talent. A speed-based EDGE who might be viewed as too one-dimensional by other teams could be a premium pick for Indianapolis — Anarumo's creative pressure packages can scheme favorable matchups for a single elite attribute. The 2024 investment in Laiatu Latu (15th overall) already addressed this need, but with Ebukam and Paye on the other side, an EDGE upgrade opposite Latu would further unlock the pressure scheme.

The coaching staff changes from 2024 to 2025 — specifically replacing Gus Bradley (predictable, Cover 3-heavy) with Anarumo (multiple, disguise-heavy) — represent a seismic shift in defensive player demands. Under Bradley, the Colts needed physical, disciplined players who could execute a simple scheme repeatedly. Under Anarumo, they need smart, versatile players who can handle weekly changes in assignment. This shift means previously undervalued attributes like football IQ, coverage versatility, and positional flexibility become premium traits in Indianapolis' draft calculus. Prospects who project as 'smart but not elite athletes' may grade higher on the Colts' board than the league average because Anarumo's scheme can compensate for athletic limitations with schematic creativity. Conversely, raw athletes who struggle with processing may grade lower — Anarumo's weekly complexity demands cognitive reliability.
